title: ONE HUNDRED YEARS OF THE CARTIER TRINITY RING
description: As Cartier's iconic three-band ring marks its centenary, we explore the love story, legacy, and luminous craftsmanship that made it immortal.
content:
In 1924, Louis Cartier presented Jean Cocteau with a ring unlike any other — three interlocking bands of yellow gold, white gold, and rose gold, each spinning freely around a common axis. It was not merely jewellery. It was a philosophy reduced to metal: love, fidelity, friendship, forever entwined.

The Trinity ring turns one hundred this year, and its century of existence has been anything but quiet. It has circled the fingers of Coco Chanel, Grace Kelly, and a generation of women who wore it precisely because it refused to be categorised. Is it casual? Ceremonial? The answer, of course, is both.

What Cartier understood intuitively was that truly great jewellery does not announce itself. It insinuates. The Trinity ring is modest in scale yet extraordinary in concept — three separate elements moving in harmony, never separating, always returning to alignment.

For its centenary year, the maison has revisited the archives and produced a series of commemorative editions: the original tricolour in polished gold, a diamond-paved interpretation that catches light from every angle, and a remarkably bold version set entirely in platinum. Each honours the original proportions. Nothing has been enlarged or dramatised. Cartier understood that there was nothing to improve.

The Trinity endures because it represents a truth about luxury that the industry occasionally forgets: the most coveted things are not the most complicated. They are the most considered.
keywords: Cartier, Trinity ring, fine jewellery, luxury, centenary, gold, heritage
